architecture.summary: ToolkenGPT augments a frozen LLM by adding learnable tool embeddings to the model's vocabulary. Each tool is represented as a discrete token (toolken) with an embedding vector concatenated to the standard token embedding matrix. During inference, the LLM operates in two modes: reasoning mode (generating text normally, with toolkens as candidate next tokens) and tool mode (when a toolken is predicted, the LLM uses in-context demonstrations to generate tool arguments, executes the external tool, and injects the result back into the sequence). The LLM parameters remain frozen; only the toolken embedding matrix is trained on paired sequences of tool calls and their outputs.
trained_components: tool-specific in-context demonstration prompts (static, not learned)

biblio.title: ToolkenGPT: Augmenting Frozen Language Models with Massive Tools via Tool Embeddings

claims.statement: ToolkenGPT enables LLMs to master massive tools without the need for any LLM fine-tuning, while still allowing for quick adaptation to new tools.
ToolkenGPT can effectively utilize arithmetic tools during the reasoning process, outperforming Chain-of-Thought and ReAct in numerical reasoning problems.
ToolkenGPT significantly outperforms baselines by training only on synthetic data when evaluating tool-handling capability with an expanded set of 13 arithmetic functions.
ToolkenGPT accommodates a substantial number of relation APIs (over 200) from a knowledge base for knowledge-based question answering.

baseline.name: in-context learning and specialized decoding methods

claims.statement: ToolkenGPT learns toolken embeddings for 58 grounded actions and objects in embodied agent task planning, offering better grounding than previous in-context learning and specialized decoding methods.
ToolkenGPT training maintains nearly the same GPU memory as LLM inference, unlike prompt tuning or prefix tuning methods.
ToolkenGPT allows extensive demonstration data for learning toolken embeddings, contrasting with in-context learning which is limited by context length.
Fine-tuning LLMs to learn tools is computationally expensive and lacks adaptability to new tools.
